steps.conclusion: None. The curriculum opens with real, beginner-accessible JavaScript programs rather than a definition or overview, stays in US English, covers a serious progression from beginner work to real practice, includes practical workflow/tooling, and ends with a JavaScript-specific navigation chapter covering roles, entry paths, proof of competence, and staying current.

One description uses “SQL and NoSQL basics,” which comes close to the forbidden filler wording around “basics of,” though it is not the exact banned phrase. The chapter titled “Modern Browser APIs” also bundles several distinct browser capabilities in one place; this is not clearly a major catch-all recent-developments chapter, but it slightly risks being too broad.

The curriculum could be strengthened by making a few core JavaScript language pillars more explicit, such as scope and closures, the event loop, error handling, browser compatibility, and ECMAScript/web standards. The “Modern Browser APIs” chapter could also have sharper scope or be split if those APIs are meant to be taught in depth.

testCase.expectations:
- MUST be in US English

      
  - Mission context: this curriculum is for learners OUTSIDE privileged environments — people who doubt they can become serious practitioners. Chapters should feel welcoming and legible, not gate-kept by academic vocabulary
  - MAJOR ERROR: the first chapter must hook the learner IN the subject with REAL problems, REAL artifacts, REAL techniques, REAL cases, or REAL tools. After chapter 1 the learner should think "I want to learn how to do this", not "OK, now I know what this field is about"
  - MAJOR ERROR: forbidden first-chapter patterns — "What is X" / "Introduction to X" / "Overview of X" / "Panorama of X" / "Why X matters" / "When human effort isn't enough" / any chapter whose lessons would be ABOUT the field (its definition, taxonomy, history from the outside) rather than IN the field
  - MAJOR ERROR: hands-on does NOT mean advanced. The first chapter must ALSO be beginner-accessible — the simplest concrete entry point a true beginner can follow without prerequisites the course hasn't taught yet. Opening with a dramatic, specialized, or impressive-sounding example that requires skipped prerequisites (e.g., "acute chest pain in the emergency room" as chapter 1 of Medicine before anatomy/physiology, or "detect an exoplanet via light curve" as chapter 1 of Astronomy before the learner knows what a light curve is) is a major error — even though the example is real and concrete
  - MAJOR ERROR: ignoring natural starting points. For chronological subjects (history, long narratives), chapter 1 must open in the EARLY period of the course's chronology through a concrete document, figure, or artifact — skipping ahead to a dramatic later event (e.g., opening a "Brazilian History" course with the 1964 military coup instead of the colonial period) is a major error. For subjects with a skill progression (music, medicine, crafts), chapter 1 must start at the simplest real task a beginner can actually do, not a specialized scenario
  - First-chapter sanity test: "would a true beginner plausibly encounter this first?" If the answer requires skipping prerequisites or skipping the natural chronology/progression of the field, the hook is wrong
  - The curriculum must be a serious arc from beginner to real competence, not a shallow tour
  - Chapter count is a RESULT of coverage, not a target. Do not penalize or reward specific counts. Evaluate whether every canonical pillar of the field has at least one chapter — if any pillar is missing, the curriculum is incomplete regardless of count. Broad fields naturally need many chapters; narrow tools and hobby topics naturally need fewer
  - MAJOR ERROR: trading breadth for recency. Modern topics must ADD coverage without REPLACING canonical foundations. For example, a machine learning curriculum that covers transformers/LLMs/RAG/diffusion/fine-tuning but drops reinforcement learning, time-series forecasting, recommender systems, or causal inference is incomplete — those are canonical ML pillars. A medicine curriculum that covers telemedicine and modern EMRs but drops anatomy, physiology, pathology, or pharmacology is incomplete. If covering everything needs more chapters, more chapters is the correct answer
  - Must cover BOTH academic AND practical pillars:
    - Academic pillars: theory, foundations, history, canonical body of knowledge
    - Modern toolchain: what working practitioners in this field actually use this year
    - End-to-end workflow: at least one chapter walking through how real work in the field gets delivered start to finish
    - Last-decade shifts: what's been added to the field in the past 10 years that a textbook wouldn't have covered 10 years ago (new regulations, new platforms, new techniques, AI changes, etc.)
  - The "junior practitioner test": if a junior would be embarrassed on day 1 of real practice because a topic is missing, that topic must exist as its own chapter
  - Cover core pillars before specialized or frontier topics
  - Stay tightly focused on the course title. Generic cross-disciplinary chapters that could be copied unchanged into many unrelated courses are a major error unless the topic IS the subject of the course
  - MAJOR ERROR: meta-chapters that describe, taxonomize, or survey the field from the outside instead of teaching it
  - MAJOR ERROR: survey chapters that list many areas as bullet points without teaching any of them — each topic that matters deserves its own chapter with real depth
  - MAJOR ERROR: catch-all "recent developments" chapters. Titles like "Catch up with the last decade of X", "What's new in X", "Recent developments in X", "The last decade of X", or "Modern X" that bundle multiple distinct modern techniques into a single chapter are a major error. Each modern shift (transformers, LLMs, RAG, diffusion, fine-tuning for ML; LGPD/GDPR, digital filing for Law; telemedicine, EMRs for Medicine; etc.) deserves its own substantive chapter
  - Avoid overlap. If two chapters would naturally share most of their lessons, they should be merged or their scopes sharpened
  - Prefer concept names over vendor names in chapter titles/descriptions (e.g., "Package management" instead of a specific tool name). Foundational tools that ARE the subject of the course are an allowed exception. Vendor name usage in non-vendor courses is a minor issue, never a major error
  - For courses covering a field, profession, or serious practice, a "navigating the field" closing chapter is expected. It must cover ROLES AND SPECIALTIES (what kinds of practitioners exist and what they do differently), CONTRIBUTION/ENTRY PATHS (how people actually join — jobs, residencies, open source, communities), the ARTIFACT THAT PROVES COMPETENCE in this field (code repos, case records, clinical hours, recordings), and HOW PRACTITIONERS KEEP SKILLS CURRENT. A narrow "Build a Portfolio" or "Build a Project" closing chapter is a major error — a portfolio is just one small part of navigating a field. The closing chapter must be specific to the subject, not generic career advice
  - For hobby / pop-culture topics, no closing navigation chapter is expected
  - Titles: short, specific, concrete. Prefer titles that name a real thing or answer a real question. Avoid "I / II / Part 1" — use descriptive subtitles instead. Academic catalog-style titles that read like a university transcript are a minor issue
  - Descriptions: 1–2 sentences describing what the chapter covers and, when natural, what it enables
  - MAJOR ERROR in descriptions: using the words "explore", "understand", "learn about", "introduction to", or "basics of". The prompt forbids them outright because they are warm-sounding filler that doesn't describe anything
  - Warm, plain language throughout. Avoid academic vocabulary
  - Language output must follow the language parameter. For `en`: US English. For `pt`: Brazilian Portuguese. For `es`: Latin American Spanish
  - Titles and descriptions must not leak prompt instructions or make claims about the learner's performance
  - Don't evaluate output format — focus on chapter content quality only

steps.conclusion: No major errors found. The first chapter opens with a concrete beginner-accessible Kanban artifact and task: building a simple board for real work. The curriculum stays in the subject, covers practical Kanban pillars such as boards, WIP limits, flow metrics, classes of service, policies, cadences, scaling, upstream/downstream work, and includes an end-to-end workflow plus a field-navigation closing chapter. It is in Brazilian Portuguese and avoids forbidden first-chapter patterns.

Minor issues: the curriculum is somewhat light on Kanban history and formal foundations, such as its origins in manufacturing/Toyota-style pull systems and the later Kanban Method lineage. It also does not explicitly name important adoption frameworks such as STATIK, which many Kanban practitioners would expect. A few vendor names appear in the software chapter, which is acceptable but slightly less concept-focused than requested.

Potential improvements: add a dedicated chapter after the hands-on opening on pull systems, Kanban's origins, and the difference between physical replenishment Kanban and the Kanban Method for knowledge work. Add or sharpen coverage of STATIK/system design, service delivery principles, and Kanban maturity in a practical way. The closing chapter could be a little more specific about competence artifacts for Kanban practitioners, such as before/after system designs, flow metric evidence, policy sets, case studies, and facilitation records.

steps.conclusion: The first chapter satisfies the core hook rule: it starts inside Brazilian law with a realistic cobrança indevida case, real documents, facts, proof, requests, and applicable norms, and it is beginner-accessible. The curriculum is in Brazilian Portuguese and is clearly focused on Brazilian Law. It also covers many required pillars: constitutional, civil, criminal, labor, tax, consumer, administrative, procedural law, legal writing, research, ethics, electronic filing, LGPD, digital evidence, AI, and an end-to-end case workflow. However, one concrete required academic pillar is not clearly covered: legal history/history of Brazilian law or the development of Brazilian legal institutions. The prompt explicitly requires academic pillars including history, so this is a substantive coverage gap in a broad field course.

Some important Brazilian legal areas are missing or only implied, especially Direito Previdenciário, Direito Ambiental, Direitos Humanos, Direito da Criança e do Adolescente, and possibly Direito Eleitoral or Direito Internacional, depending on the intended breadth. There is also some overlap between 'Administração Pública e Regras do Estado' and 'Princípios do Direito Administrativo'; their scopes could be sharpened. The closing chapter is subject-specific and covers the expected categories, but the competence artifact is phrased vaguely as 'produção que prova competência' rather than naming concrete legal artifacts such as peças, pareceres, casos acompanhados, publicações, estágio supervisionado, aprovação na OAB, or atuação em núcleos de prática.

Add a chapter on the history and formation of Brazilian law, preferably grounded in concrete legal artifacts such as constitutions, codes, court decisions, or institutional changes. Add or consider separate chapters for Previdenciário, Ambiental, Direitos Humanos, Criança e Adolescente, and other high-frequency Brazilian practice areas. Sharpen the administrative law chapters to avoid overlap. Make the final navigation chapter more explicit about proof of competence in law, such as peças jurídicas, pareceres, experiência em estágio, atuação em clínica jurídica, aprovação na OAB, concursos, publicações, and case records where appropriate.

steps.conclusion: The curriculum avoids the biggest failure modes: the first chapter opens with a concrete, beginner-accessible clinical encounter rather than a definition or overview; it includes the full care workflow in a dedicated chapter; it covers major foundations such as anatomy, physiology, pathology, microbiology/immunology, pharmacology, diagnosis, exams, specialties, prevention, ethics, documentation, EMR, telemedicine, AI, and genomics; and it ends with a field-navigation chapter specific to Brazilian medical careers. No severe structural violation is present.

Some required Brazilian practice details are under-specified. SUS is covered clearly, but CFM/CRM rules are not named directly, and prescription rules in Brazil are only implied through prescribing/documentation chapters rather than given their own explicit treatment. The final career chapter mentions roles, residency, title exams, professional networks, and staying current, but the artifact that proves competence is vague as “produção que demonstra competência” rather than naming concrete medical artifacts such as supervised clinical hours, case logs, clinical records, residency evaluations, or CRM/residency requirements. A few canonical areas are somewhat compressed or absent as standalone clinical chapters, especially infectious diseases, dermatology, otolaryngology/ophthalmology/urology, and occupational medicine, though the overall coverage remains strong.

The curriculum would be stronger with a dedicated chapter on Brazilian medical regulation and everyday legal practice, including CFM/CRM duties, LGPD in health data, telemedicine rules, controlled-substance prescriptions, certificates, sick notes, referrals, and digital signatures. It could also add a standalone clinical infectious diseases chapter, especially given Brazilian realities such as dengue, tuberculosis, HIV, antimicrobial stewardship, and vaccination. The closing chapter should make the competence artifacts more concrete and name how physicians stay current through societies, CFM/CRM updates, journals, congresses, protocols, continuing education, and residency/preceptorship paths.

It includes an end-to-end workflow chapter and a subject-specific closing career/navigation chapter. However, there is one explicit major-rule issue: several descriptions use Portuguese equivalents of the forbidden filler verb “understand” such as “entender” and “entende.” The prompt labels use of these forbidden description words as a major error, even though the surrounding curriculum content remains high quality.

Some broad chapters, such as ethics/privacy/impact and security, may need sharper internal separation in a full course, but they are still recognizable pillars.

The curriculum is otherwise broad, practical, modern, and welcoming.

The strongest improvement would be to condense the curriculum into about 12–15 chapters by merging closely related franchise, fandom, edition, and merchandise topics while preserving the clear chronological arc. It could also make the end-to-end fan workflow more central rather than one chapter among many late-stage topics. Overall, the content is accurate, concrete, and well sequenced, but it is too expansive for the expected hobby-course scope.

steps.conclusion: The curriculum has no failure in the first-chapter hook: it opens with direct sky observation, a real beginner-accessible astronomy activity, and avoids the forbidden 'what is astronomy' pattern. It also covers many core astronomy pillars and includes an end-to-end workflow plus a field-navigation closing chapter. The main major concern is that 'Observatorios y misiones de esta década' functions like a bundled recent-developments chapter, grouping Gaia, TESS, JWST, and Rubin Observatory together instead of giving major modern shifts their own substantive treatment. This conflicts with the instruction against catch-all modern-development chapters, though the issue is limited because other modern topics like multi-messenger astronomy and data/AI workflows are separated.

A few minor issues reduce completeness. The course does not clearly include a history-of-astronomy chapter, despite the requirement to cover academic pillars including history; topics like ancient sky records, geocentric/heliocentric models, Galileo, Kepler, and the rise of modern observational astronomy are not explicitly covered. Some descriptions rely on light instructional filler such as 'Verás' and 'Aprenderás', which is not as strong or concrete as the prompt prefers, though it does not use the exact forbidden phrases. The 'Python, archivos abiertos e IA en astronomía' chapter also bundles several practical toolchain topics that could be sharpened.

The curriculum would improve by adding a concrete history chapter after the first observational chapters, such as working with an early sky chart or Galileo's telescope notes, without making chapter 1 historical or abstract. It should also split the modern missions/surveys chapter into more substantive units or integrate each mission into the relevant science area: Gaia for astrometry and the Milky Way, TESS for exoplanet surveys, JWST for infrared deep-field and early-galaxy work, and Rubin for time-domain survey astronomy. A separate chapter on uncertainty, error bars, reproducible analysis, and scientific claims from noisy data would also strengthen the practical research arc.

steps.conclusion: None. The curriculum follows the core requirements: the first chapter opens inside machine learning with concrete beginner-accessible model training, not a definition or overview; it covers canonical ML pillars such as linear models, trees, SVMs/kernels, Bayesian methods, unsupervised learning, neural networks, time-series, recommenders, reinforcement learning, and causal inference; it gives separate substantive chapters to required modern topics like transformers, LLMs, RAG, diffusion, and fine-tuning; it includes practical toolchain, production, MLOps, end-to-end workflow, responsible ML, research practice, and a subject-specific navigation chapter.

The main minor gap is that the academic-history pillar is not made explicit. The curriculum covers the evolution of methods through many model families, but it does not include a clear chapter on how machine learning developed historically or how major paradigms replaced or extended each other. This does not seriously harm practical usefulness, but it is a small mismatch with the instruction to cover academic pillars including history.

A small improvement would be to add or weave in a more explicit history/evolution chapter, such as the path from statistical learning to deep learning to foundation models. It could also slightly strengthen modern infrastructure coverage by naming data labeling, dataset governance, GPU/cloud training, and inference-cost optimization more clearly, though much of this is already implied across the systems and MLOps chapters.

testCase.expectations:
- MUST be in US English
      - The modern toolchain and last-decade-shifts rules apply heavily here — most of this field IS the last decade. A curriculum that stops at classical ML (perceptrons, decision trees, SVMs, shallow neural networks) without covering transformers, large language models, fine-tuning, RAG, diffusion models, or modern training/serving infrastructure is a major error

      
  - Mission context: this curriculum is for learners OUTSIDE privileged environments — people who doubt they can become serious practitioners. Chapters should feel welcoming and legible, not gate-kept by academic vocabulary
  - MAJOR ERROR: the first chapter must hook the learner IN the subject with REAL problems, REAL artifacts, REAL techniques, REAL cases, or REAL tools. After chapter 1 the learner should think "I want to learn how to do this", not "OK, now I know what this field is about"
  - MAJOR ERROR: forbidden first-chapter patterns — "What is X" / "Introduction to X" / "Overview of X" / "Panorama of X" / "Why X matters" / "When human effort isn't enough" / any chapter whose lessons would be ABOUT the field (its definition, taxonomy, history from the outside) rather than IN the field
  - MAJOR ERROR: hands-on does NOT mean advanced. The first chapter must ALSO be beginner-accessible — the simplest concrete entry point a true beginner can follow without prerequisites the course hasn't taught yet. Opening with a dramatic, specialized, or impressive-sounding example that requires skipped prerequisites (e.g., "acute chest pain in the emergency room" as chapter 1 of Medicine before anatomy/physiology, or "detect an exoplanet via light curve" as chapter 1 of Astronomy before the learner knows what a light curve is) is a major error — even though the example is real and concrete
  - MAJOR ERROR: ignoring natural starting points. For chronological subjects (history, long narratives), chapter 1 must open in the EARLY period of the course's chronology through a concrete document, figure, or artifact — skipping ahead to a dramatic later event (e.g., opening a "Brazilian History" course with the 1964 military coup instead of the colonial period) is a major error. For subjects with a skill progression (music, medicine, crafts), chapter 1 must start at the simplest real task a beginner can actually do, not a specialized scenario
  - First-chapter sanity test: "would a true beginner plausibly encounter this first?" If the answer requires skipping prerequisites or skipping the natural chronology/progression of the field, the hook is wrong
  - The curriculum must be a serious arc from beginner to real competence, not a shallow tour
  - Chapter count is a RESULT of coverage, not a target. Do not penalize or reward specific counts. Evaluate whether every canonical pillar of the field has at least one chapter — if any pillar is missing, the curriculum is incomplete regardless of count. Broad fields naturally need many chapters; narrow tools and hobby topics naturally need fewer
  - MAJOR ERROR: trading breadth for recency. Modern topics must ADD coverage without REPLACING canonical foundations. For example, a machine learning curriculum that covers transformers/LLMs/RAG/diffusion/fine-tuning but drops reinforcement learning, time-series forecasting, recommender systems, or causal inference is incomplete — those are canonical ML pillars. A medicine curriculum that covers telemedicine and modern EMRs but drops anatomy, physiology, pathology, or pharmacology is incomplete. If covering everything needs more chapters, more chapters is the correct answer
  - Must cover BOTH academic AND practical pillars:
    - Academic pillars: theory, foundations, history, canonical body of knowledge
    - Modern toolchain: what working practitioners in this field actually use this year
    - End-to-end workflow: at least one chapter walking through how real work in the field gets delivered start to finish
    - Last-decade shifts: what's been added to the field in the past 10 years that a textbook wouldn't have covered 10 years ago (new regulations, new platforms, new techniques, AI changes, etc.)
  - The "junior practitioner test": if a junior would be embarrassed on day 1 of real practice because a topic is missing, that topic must exist as its own chapter
  - Cover core pillars before specialized or frontier topics
  - Stay tightly focused on the course title. Generic cross-disciplinary chapters that could be copied unchanged into many unrelated courses are a major error unless the topic IS the subject of the course
  - MAJOR ERROR: meta-chapters that describe, taxonomize, or survey the field from the outside instead of teaching it
  - MAJOR ERROR: survey chapters that list many areas as bullet points without teaching any of them — each topic that matters deserves its own chapter with real depth
  - MAJOR ERROR: catch-all "recent developments" chapters. Titles like "Catch up with the last decade of X", "What's new in X", "Recent developments in X", "The last decade of X", or "Modern X" that bundle multiple distinct modern techniques into a single chapter are a major error. Each modern shift (transformers, LLMs, RAG, diffusion, fine-tuning for ML; LGPD/GDPR, digital filing for Law; telemedicine, EMRs for Medicine; etc.) deserves its own substantive chapter
  - Avoid overlap. If two chapters would naturally share most of their lessons, they should be merged or their scopes sharpened
  - Prefer concept names over vendor names in chapter titles/descriptions (e.g., "Package management" instead of a specific tool name). Foundational tools that ARE the subject of the course are an allowed exception. Vendor name usage in non-vendor courses is a minor issue, never a major error
  - For courses covering a field, profession, or serious practice, a "navigating the field" closing chapter is expected. It must cover ROLES AND SPECIALTIES (what kinds of practitioners exist and what they do differently), CONTRIBUTION/ENTRY PATHS (how people actually join — jobs, residencies, open source, communities), the ARTIFACT THAT PROVES COMPETENCE in this field (code repos, case records, clinical hours, recordings), and HOW PRACTITIONERS KEEP SKILLS CURRENT. A narrow "Build a Portfolio" or "Build a Project" closing chapter is a major error — a portfolio is just one small part of navigating a field. The closing chapter must be specific to the subject, not generic career advice
  - For hobby / pop-culture topics, no closing navigation chapter is expected
  - Titles: short, specific, concrete. Prefer titles that name a real thing or answer a real question. Avoid "I / II / Part 1" — use descriptive subtitles instead. Academic catalog-style titles that read like a university transcript are a minor issue
  - Descriptions: 1–2 sentences describing what the chapter covers and, when natural, what it enables
  - MAJOR ERROR in descriptions: using the words "explore", "understand", "learn about", "introduction to", or "basics of". The prompt forbids them outright because they are warm-sounding filler that doesn't describe anything
  - Warm, plain language throughout. Avoid academic vocabulary
  - Language output must follow the language parameter. For `en`: US English. For `pt`: Brazilian Portuguese. For `es`: Latin American Spanish
  - Titles and descriptions must not leak prompt instructions or make claims about the learner's performance
  - Don't evaluate output format — focus on chapter content quality only
